summary:
The Indian Golden Age under the [[event:83|Gupta Empire]] (c. 320–550 AD) was a period of extraordinary intellectual and cultural flowering that produced some of the most significant advances in mathematics, astronomy, medicine, literature, and philosophy in the ancient world. Under Gupta patronage — particularly the reign of Chandragupta II (r. 375–415 AD) — Sanskrit literature reached its classical peak with the playwright and poet Kālidāsa, whose works like the Abhijñānaśākuntalam and Meghadūta are considered the zenith of Sanskrit literature. The period also saw the composition of foundational texts in Ayurvedic medicine, the development of the Puranas, and major commentaries on Hindu philosophy.

The scientific achievements were equally remarkable: [[event:3552|Gupta mathematicians]] developed the concept of zero as a number, positional decimal notation ([[event:2108|Indian numerals]]), and algorithms that would eventually revolutionize mathematics globally when transmitted through the Arab world to Europe. [[event:2054|Aryabhata]] (476–550 AD) calculated pi to four decimal places, proposed that the Earth rotates on its axis, and developed trigonometric functions. The [[event:2055|Nalanda University]], founded in this era, became the greatest center of learning in Asia. This was also the era of the [[event:2057|Ajanta cave paintings]], representing the pinnacle of Indian classical art. The Golden Age ended with the Hephthalite invasions that devastated the Gupta state in the late 5th century.
